entities.name: dharma
entities.cite: where the fruit is unseen: in dharma

entities.name: sādhu
entities.cite: correct words are to be spoken, by the cultivated

axioms.name: extends
axioms.cite: let the same be acknowledged where the fruit is unseen: in dharma

axioms.name: sadhu
axioms.cite: correct words are to be spoken, by the cultivated

axioms.name: enables
axioms.cite: for those (or: by those) who seek abhyudaya, felicity, the rising good

claims.name: extends
claims.cite: so let it be acknowledged with respect to dharma as well

claims.name: sadhu
claims.cite: what is correct is to be spoken

rejected_readings.label: form_indifference
rejected_readings.rendering: Correct and incorrect word-forms are equally efficacious in pursuing dharma.
rejected_readings.why: Denies that samarthya (capacity/power) is form-bound. The verse's doctrine holds that power resides specifically in sadhu (correct) forms. Incorrect forms (asadhu) lack this power and cannot efficaciously support dharmic aims.

rejected_readings.label: domain_restriction
rejected_readings.rendering: Word-forms have demonstrable causal efficacy in the visible domain (poison cures illness), but dharmic effects operate through different metaphysical principles; the form-bound power of language cannot be transferred to the unseen realm.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's foundational inference extends samarthya from the visible domain (drṣṭa, with perceptible poison-cure effects) to the unseen domain (adrṣṭa, dharma with invisible fruits). The commentary explicitly identifies this as 'the standard extension from drṣṭa to adrṣṭa efficacy that ritual reasoning always makes.' This reading denies that extension, treating samarthya as domain-specific to visible phenomena. It fails because it rejects the cross-domain transferability that grounds the entire ethical doctrine.

notes: Verse 1.139 extends the doctrine of words' form-bound power (established in 1.138) to the ethical domain (dharma). The logical move: if samarthya (capacity) operates demonstrably in the visible domain, then equally in the unseen domain of dharma. This form-binding makes grammar into ethics—correctness is not mere convention but efficacious practice toward abhyudaya (felicity/the rising good).
